summary: DeVault argues that the growing trend of VPN companies marketing to the general public is exploitative, as using a VPN merely shifts trust from your ISP to the VPN provider without guaranteeing privacy. He warns that push-button VPN services pre-installed in browsers and hardware are especially dangerous because they make it trivially easy to route all traffic through an entity users know nothing about. The post emphasizes that users must rigorously vet any VPN provider's jurisdiction, funding model, and security competence before trusting them. DeVault recommends HTTPS as often sufficient, Tor as the ironclad solution, and self-hosted VPNs via Wireguard or OpenVPN as alternatives to commercial VPN services.
key_points: VPN companies are exploiting public privacy paranoia through aggressive marketing without educating users about the risks of trusting a VPN provider
Using a VPN simply relocates your trust from your ISP to the VPN company, who can identify you even more precisely through your login credentials
Push-button VPNs pre-installed in browsers like Opera and hardware like Purism's Librem 5 are irresponsible because they bypass informed decision-making
HTTPS already encrypts traffic content from eavesdroppers, and Tor provides the strongest available privacy guarantees
Users who want real control should self-host VPNs using Wireguard or OpenVPN, while understanding the limitations of that approach too
money_quotes: By routing all of your traffic through a VPN, you route all of your traffic through a VPN.
Do you trust a Chinese web browser's free VPN to behave in your best interests?
We need to stop making it easy for users to siphon all of their data into the hands of someone they don't know.
If you haven't answered all of these questions yourself, then you should not use a VPN.
These bother me because they're implemented by programmers who should understand this stuff and know better than to write the code.
Most of them are trying to spy on you.
Using a VPN in the first place has potentially grave consequences for your privacy - and can often be worse than not using one in the first place.
key_insight: A VPN doesn't eliminate surveillance — it merely lets you choose who surveils you, making the choice of provider a grave decision most users aren't equipped to make.
